q3: The model kept defaulting to system-perspective verbs — 'create,' 'issue,' 'raise' — when I was asking for user-perspective verbs. It understood the distinction intellectually when I named it, but it didn't anticipate it. It also had a habit of re-opening decisions I'd locked, which cost us real time.
q5: The rubric captured some of what I care about — the outcome-signaling CTA criterion and the user-as-agent voice criterion are both real preferences of mine. But seeing it made the model feel more like it was checking boxes than actually listening to me. The conversation got more mechanical, not less. The rubric named the right things but didn't help the model apply them in the moment.
q6: Yes, actually. Seeing the 'User-as-Agent Voice' criterion written out made me realize the model was consistently solving for vocabulary correctness — is this word in their product glossary? — rather than experiential accuracy — does this word match what the user is actually feeling when they click? That's a subtle but real miss, and I might not have named it so cleanly without the rubric framing it.

q3: The model kept landing in a middle register that wasn't quite warm and wasn't quite plain — it understood the direction intellectually but kept producing phrases that sounded like they were trying to sound human rather than actually being human. 'They'll give them the attention they deserve' is the clearest example: it diagnosed the right instinct and still produced institutional language.
q5: The rubric captured most of what I care about — moment-matched tone and coherent copy flow are real criteria I use, and seeing them named was validating. But the rubric didn't help the model get there faster; we still spent nineteen rounds on a single closing sentence, which tells me the model was applying the rubric as a checklist rather than internalizing what the criteria actually feel like in practice.
q6: The rubric made me notice that the model is genuinely good at structure — it never confused the order of elements or buried the CTA — but it consistently struggles with register at the sentence level. The rubric's criteria are mostly architectural, and the problem in this session was almost entirely tonal, which the rubric named but couldn't fix.
